architecture_patterns.mapping: Create a small root atlas, link child maps by subsystem boundary, and regenerate reports in CI.
architecture_patterns.name: Map-of-maps for huge repositories
architecture_patterns.redaction: Publish path-level maps, sanitized contracts, and review hints.
architecture_patterns.works_for: Monorepos, kernels, platform repos, multi-language systems

architecture_patterns.mapping: Run atlas scan/build in CI, diff the generated report, and route changed subsystem maps to reviewers.
architecture_patterns.name: Merge-time system drift check
architecture_patterns.redaction: Keep private data and secrets out of manifests; expose interfaces only.
architecture_patterns.works_for: Projects that want architecture reports on every merge

bigger_picture: This is a large-repository map-of-maps and blueprint stress test for System Review Graph. It uses real Linux kernel tracked paths plus source-evidence anchors to show the whole-system shape: build/config, boot/init, process scheduling, syscall entry, memory, filesystem/block IO, networking, driver probing, security hooks, modules/BPF/tracing, and Rust integration. The target reading experience is a blueprint wall: start with the root structure, then drill into the source-backed flow and child maps. It is still not an official Linux maintainer audit and does not prove runtime behavior without tests/traces.

control_points.failure_mode: feature absent from build or dependency conflict

control_points.decision: in-tree build or external module build
control_points.failure_mode: external path invalid or incompatible with config

known_boundaries: This blueprint maps source/build control surfaces, not a specific .config or compiled image.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Shows how the top-level Linux source tree becomes configured build intent instead of treating directories as isolated folders.
review_questions: Which top-level directories are config-selected into the build?
Which generated headers or scripts become hidden contracts for later source paths?
Which subsystem is absent in this atlas because it is build-generated rather than source-tree visible?

source_evidence.notes: Top-level config includes helper macros first.
Top-level Kconfig fans out to init, kernel, fs, mm, net, drivers, security, crypto, lib, docs, and io_uring.
Main build supports external module roots and exports that path.
Kconfig tooling turns config targets into .config decisions.

blueprint_sections.title: Whole Repository Coverage And Build Configuration

control_points.failure_mode: dependency starts too early or too late
no init process or rootfs issue

flow.consumes: early memory, CPU, scheduler, IRQ, console state

flow.step: Run late init and userspace handoff

known_boundaries: This section uses generic source anchors and does not map every architecture boot file.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Tracks the early kernel path from architecture handoff into generic kernel init and the first userspace process.
review_questions: Which initcalls are required before filesystems, drivers, and userspace can run?
Which boot path is architecture-specific and which is generic?
Where would a boot failure be proven by logs or tracepoints?

source_evidence.notes: Generic initialization begins here after architecture handoff.
Creates/continues the path toward kernel_init and idle scheduling.
Runs the late init path and userspace transition logic.
Built-in init functions are ordered through initcall classes.

blueprint_sections.title: Boot, Kernel Init, And Userspace Handoff

control_points.failure_mode: invalid flags, limits, namespace, or resource failure
latency, starvation, or priority inversion if invariants break
lost wakeup or wrong runqueue placement

flow.consumes: task exit code and owned resources
flow.produces: released task state and parent notification

known_boundaries: This section does not expand every scheduler class, CPU topology path, or architecture context switch implementation.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Maps how tasks are created, made runnable, selected, switched, woken, and exited.
review_questions: Which scheduler class owns the task at each moment?
Which locks or barriers protect wakeup and task state?
Which tests or traces prove latency and fairness for a specific workload?

source_evidence.notes: Core task creation path used under clone/kernel_clone flows.
Turns clone arguments into a task creation request.
Main scheduling call path.
Chooses the next task via scheduler class logic.
Moves blocked/sleeping tasks toward runnable state.
Core task exit path.

blueprint_sections.title: Process Lifetime And Scheduler Control Plane

control_points.decision: valid syscall number and ABI route
control_points.failure_mode: not implemented or wrong ABI dispatch
control_points.location: arch syscall tables and entry code

control_points.failure_mode: EFAULT or unsafe memory access

flow.evidence: arch entry surface plus syscall macros

known_boundaries: Architecture-specific syscall table files are not fully expanded in this root blueprint.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Shows how user requests enter kernel-defined syscall contracts and land in subsystem implementations.
review_questions: Which architecture entry file maps the syscall number to the generic implementation?
Which subsystem owns the post-syscall object model?
Where is user memory copied, checked, or faulted?

source_evidence.notes: Defines syscall macro family and generated signatures.
Read syscall delegates into kernel read helper logic.
Public read syscall wrapper.
Public write syscall wrapper.
Represents not-implemented syscall handling.

blueprint_sections.title: User-Kernel Boundary And Syscall Dispatch

control_points.failure_mode: permission fault or bad mapping
OOM kill, allocation failure, latency spike

known_boundaries: Slab, vmalloc, huge pages, memcg, NUMA, and architecture fault entry are not fully expanded here.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Connects mmap/VMA decisions, page faults, allocator fast/slow paths, reclaim/compaction/OOM pressure, and exported memory contracts.
review_questions: Which memory path is user mapping, kernel allocation, slab, vmalloc, or page cache?
Which GFP flags and context determine blocking behavior?
Which tracepoints prove allocation pressure in runtime?

source_evidence.notes: Creates userland memory mappings.
Core page fault handling path.
Exported fault handling wrapper.
Handles difficult allocation cases.
Core page allocation entry.

blueprint_sections.title: Memory Management, Mappings, Faults, And Page Allocation

control_points.failure_mode: ENOENT, permissions, RCU lookup fallback
EACCES or LSM denial
IO latency, congestion, device error

flow.consumes: struct file, user buffer, count, position

flow.next: block layer if backed by block device

known_boundaries: Specific filesystem implementations such as ext4, btrfs, xfs, and network filesystems need child blueprints.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Shows how file-oriented syscalls move through path lookup, file objects, VFS read/write, filesystem operations, and block-device submission.
review_questions: Which filesystem file_operations implementation is reached for a specific path?
Does the path hit page cache, direct IO, or block IO?
Which LSM or mount namespace decision can stop the operation?

source_evidence.notes: Turns userspace open request into kernel open flow.
Core path resolution/open helper.
VFS read entry once a file is resolved.
VFS write entry once a file is resolved.
Submits BIO requests to block layer.
Creates/sends block requests.

blueprint_sections.title: Filesystem, VFS, Path Lookup, And Block IO

control_points.failure_mode: drop, wrong handler, filter path

control_points.decision: accept, route, deliver, or drop packet
control_points.failure_mode: invalid header, checksum, policy, congestion
device queue pressure or qdisc drop

known_boundaries: IPv6, UDP, netfilter, namespaces, routing tables, qdisc, and NIC-specific drivers need child blueprints.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Maps packets from network device buffers through core receive, IP/TCP dispatch, and transmit queue submission.
review_questions: Which driver/NAPI path creates the skb?
Which netfilter, BPF, or qdisc hooks can intercept this packet?
Which tracepoints prove drops versus delivery?

source_evidence.notes: Main receive data processing function.
Core packet receive path.
IPv4 packet receive handler.
TCP over IPv4 receive handler.
Transmits skb toward device queue.

blueprint_sections.title: Networking Packet Receive, Protocol Dispatch, And Transmit

control_points.failure_mode: device remains unbound
probe error, deferred probe, broken device

known_boundaries: PCI, USB, platform, ACPI, device-tree, and individual driver families need child blueprints.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Shows how drivers register with buses, devices match to drivers, and probe binds runtime device behavior.
review_questions: Which bus-specific match function controls this device?
What resources are acquired during probe and released on remove?
Which deferred-probe or firmware dependency blocks binding?

source_evidence.notes: Registers driver and passes work to bus_add_driver.
Adds a driver to a bus.
Core device-driver binding/probe function.
Attempts to bind device and driver together.
Tries to attach a device to a driver.

blueprint_sections.title: Driver Model, Device Matching, And Probe

control_points.decision: allow, deny, annotate, or audit operation
control_points.failure_mode: EACCES/EPERM or policy denial

control_points.decision: which operations can be controlled by LSMs
control_points.failure_mode: operation has no hook or wrong hook semantics

known_boundaries: SELinux, AppArmor, Smack, Landlock, BPF LSM, IMA/EVM, and capabilities need separate child blueprints.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Maps the reusable security hook layer that lets security modules gate file, inode, process, network, BPF, and other kernel operations.
review_questions: Which security_* wrapper is called by the subsystem path?
Which configured LSM implementations register the hook?
Is the operation denied, audited, or only labeled?

source_evidence.notes: Defines the hook inventory used by LSM infrastructure.
One concrete filesystem permission hook.
Dispatches int-returning LSM hooks.
Calls inode_permission hook implementations.
Docs mention inode permission as an example hook.

blueprint_sections.title: Security And LSM Permission Hooks

control_points.decision: accept, reject, initialize, or fail module
control_points.failure_mode: bad signature, bad format, unresolved symbol, init failure

control_points.decision: program accepted and attachable or rejected
control_points.failure_mode: verifier failure, security_bpf_prog_load denial

known_boundaries: Module signing, livepatch, perf, ftrace, kprobes, uprobes, and BPF verifier internals need child blueprints.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Shows how Linux extends runtime behavior through loadable modules, initcall/module_init surfaces, BPF program loading, and tracepoint/observability surfaces.
review_questions: Which runtime behavior is built in, module-loaded, or BPF-attached?
Which security and signature checks gate extension loading?
Which tracepoints prove this path at runtime?

source_evidence.notes: Drivers and modules declare initialization entry points.
Core module loading implementation.
Userspace module load syscall path.
Loads module from file descriptor path.
Loads/verifies BPF programs.
Userspace BPF command syscall.

blueprint_sections.title: Extensibility, Modules, BPF, And Tracing

control_points.failure_mode: toolchain mismatch or config disabled

control_points.decision: wrap C kernel APIs safely enough for Rust caller
control_points.failure_mode: incorrect wrapper invariants or unsafe contract violation

flow.consumes: Rust abstractions and kernel module model

known_boundaries: This blueprint does not inspect each Rust abstraction, unsafe block, or Rust driver implementation.
blueprint_sections.purpose: Maps Rust as a kernel language surface: config-gated availability, build integration, generated bindings, safe abstractions, and driver/sample integration.
review_questions: Which Rust wrappers encapsulate unsafe C/kernel behavior?
Which generated bindings are relied on by a driver?
Does the driver enter normal module/driver probe paths after Rust build integration?

source_evidence.notes: Rust support is configured under general setup.
Top-level build changes behavior when Rust is enabled.
Rust core/helpers/bindings/uapi/kernel objects are config-gated.
Docs describe what Rust support can link.
Docs describe generated bindings and wrappers around unsafe functionality.

blueprint_sections.title: Rust Kernel Integration And Safety Boundary

current_truth.blueprint_limit: major flows and control planes, not every file or every architecture variant
current_truth.map_strategy: root atlas plus linked child subsystem manifests

decision_gates.name: Subsystem Review Gate
decision_gates.risk_boundary: Atlas output is a navigation structure and source-surface hypothesis, not proof of runtime behavior.

rules.if: child map lacks workflows or gates

known_boundaries: Atlas boundaries are inferred from source-tree directories and markers.
A child map gives review context; it does not prove runtime behavior by itself.
Very large systems still need maintainers or deeper agents to refine real workflows.
CI regeneration can detect drift, but deciding meaning still needs review gates.
The Linux example was generated from a path-only mirror of the git tree; file contents, build configuration, runtime behavior, and maintainer ownership were not audited.
Blueprint sections cover major Linux operational flows, but not every file, architecture variant, driver family, filesystem, network protocol, scheduler class, or security module implementation.
one_line: Map-of-maps generated for a large repository: root atlas links to subsystem manifests.

rebuild_recipe.goal: Generate root and child subsystem maps.
Regenerate reports in CI after a merge or major milestone.

review_questions: Which child maps changed since the last merge?
Which subsystem boundary is too broad and needs splitting?
Which subsystem lacks workflow, gate, schema, or test evidence?
Can reviewers reproduce the map from the declared rebuild recipe?
Where does the atlas overclaim beyond source-surface evidence?

schemas.name: SubsystemMap
schemas.privacy_notes: Only path-level source metadata is included by default.
schemas.purpose: A child system review map that a root atlas can link, upload, and hand to reviewers or agents.

scope: Generated from Linux kernel git tree paths at commit 2d3090a; source blobs were not copied.

source_links.label: Linux kernel repository
source_links.notes: Public source repository used for the path-tree stress test.

title: Linux Kernel System Review Atlas

review: Open changed child manifests under reports/system-review/subsystems/
Promote accepted inferred maps into reviewed manifests

walkthroughs.name: Review a huge repository without flattening it
walkthroughs.story: Start at the atlas report, choose the subsystem that changed or looks risky, open the child manifest, then refine that child into exact workflows and gates.

workflows.name: Scan Root Boundaries
workflows.purpose: Detect top-level subsystem boundaries and generate child maps.

workflows.name: Open Child Maps
workflows.purpose: Load child manifests to understand each subsystem without losing the root map.

workflows.name: Refine Atlas
workflows.purpose: Replace inferred boundaries with reviewed workflows, APIs, and gates.

workflows.name: Regenerate On Merge
workflows.purpose: Keep the atlas current after merges so reviewers can see system drift, new features, and changed review targets.
